title: Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita, 2024
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and the typical daily standard of living for individuals across the globe in 2024. The horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale, while the vertical axis shows median income or consumption per day, also in 2021 prices. Circle sizes correspond to population, meaning larger bubbles represent more populous nations. A strong positive association is visible, where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher median daily income or consumption. At the lower end of the distribution, countries in sub-Saharan Africa such as Burundi, DR Congo, and Madagascar cluster in the bottom-left, with GDP per capita below $2,000 and median daily figures around or below $2. Moving toward the center, populous Asian nations like India and Pakistan sit in the $5 to $10 daily range with GDP per capita between $5,000 and $10,000. China and Vietnam appear higher, with median daily values between $10 and $20. European and North American nations occupy the upper-right quadrant; the United States shows the highest values on both axes, exceeding $50,000 in GDP per capita and $50 in daily median income. Japan and other European countries cluster tightly in this high-income band. The data excludes international aviation and relies on income measures after taxes and benefits or consumption expenditure depending on the country. The visual pattern shows a consistent upward slope where increases in national wealth align with increases in the median individual's daily resources.
